text: %d ticks passed while you were away

Day three. The soil here is soft. The more you dig, the more you earn — good work, honest work. But the foreman says strange things. "Train your ears before you train your depth," he tells us. Whatever that means. Last night, from somewhere far below the shaft, came a sound — not quite water, not quite breathing. An aquifer, everyone laughed. I laughed too.

Dear brother. Thank you for the money. The site here is the same as ever. There is just one odd rule: "Any stone tablet you find, hand it in unread." They say the ones who read them are the ones who quit. The others laugh — you can't even read, you're the safest man in the pit. ...But brother. If a man cannot read, how is it that in his dreams he finds himself *reading* it anyway?

[LEGIBLE SECTION] ...to appease it, we ceased our digging. Seven times we filled the shaft back in, and seven times we failed. Return the soil, stack the stones — it does not matter. The depth remembers. You may forget, but the hole does not. ...[REMAINDER LOST]

I am beginning to understand what the foreman means by training your ears. Deep down, the sound of your pickaxe comes back to you — one beat late, and somehow a little more polite. I took it for an echo at first. Then Yorek tried it: three strikes, then stop. It answered with four. The foreman said: "Don't try to match its count. As long as it matches yours, the work goes well." And in fact, they say this month's yield is the highest on record.
Torn Page from a Wage Ledger

[Ledger, excerpt] Week 7: Daron, 20 silver. Yorek, 20 silver. Mim, 25 (night shift). Week 8: Daron, 40. Yorek, 40. Note: wages doubled for all work below the tenth stratum. Week 9: Daron, 80. Note: wages quadrupled. Held in storage, recipient absent. Week 10: Note: we are running out of storage.

We reached the aquifer today. The water is clean, and cold, and it flows upward. That is not a slip of the pen. It climbs the walls in quiet, orderly lines, up toward the ceiling. Daron said there must be a lake on top of the mountain. We laughed. I have noticed, lately, that we measure our fear by how often we laugh. Today we laughed a great deal.

[NOTICE No. 11] The following are prohibited: 1. Singing in the shafts. 2. Whistling in the shafts. 3. Deliberately breaking the rhythm of the work. 4. Striking back in reply to the Answer. 5. Explaining the reasons for the above when a newcomer asks. — Those who know the reasons already know how to answer. — The Foreman

[Royal Bureau of Mines — Charter] The 170th petition to resume excavation is hereby GRANTED, on the following conditions: the workforce shall not exceed one hundred; no open flame shall be used below the fortieth stratum; the decision to withdraw rests entirely with the site foreman, and the capital need not be notified until the withdrawal is complete. — Note: the Bureau is obliged to record that all one hundred and sixty-nine previous petitions were written in the same hand.

(Scratched into the bedrock wall with a nail, or something like one) HERE SLEEPS MIM. a lie. not sleeping. awake. always awake. keep quiet and the work is easy. don't sing. don't count. don't answer. — And beneath that, one line in a different hand: "Mim is doing fine. Please don't worry."

We were not the first. In the thirty-fourth stratum we broke into scaffolding that was not ours, and the ruins of an archive that was not ours. The tree rings in the timber date it to twelve hundred years ago. Here the journal changes. The pages that follow are in our own surveyor's handwriting — but in letters none of us know. Only the final line can be read: "The rest is below."

[Inscription in the stone chamber] The digger shall be filled. That is the promise of this place. Soil into silver, silver doubled, the double doubled again. Seek the depth. Yield not the depth. ...Here the inscription breaks off, scraped away. On examination, the tool that scraped it was the same tool that carved it.

Yorek quit. To be precise, he stopped coming to morning roll. His pack is still here. His bunk is still here. His wage pouch is still here. The foreman sent out no search party. All he said was: "Those who go down are employed down there." I took it for a joke, and laughed. Nobody laughed. Since this month, for some reason, there are two wage pouches with my name on them.

Dear brother. You will recall I wrote that I cannot read. I can now. I do not remember learning. One morning I woke, and the marks on the tablets looked like plain dictation — just sounds, written down. They say it happens to everyone here. One of the old hands puts it differently: "You haven't learned the tunnel's language. It has learned yours." Either way, the pay is good. Only — these days, in my dreams, I find I am writing my letters to you in those marks.
